The problem, however, is none of these three parts have the power to get you off the gravitational pull of suffering or long-term stress. And we succumb back into the vortex of habitual stressful emotions. We need a greater inner power to get us to a Beautiful State. And that is the way of inner truth. About eight years ago, we as a family met with an accident while at Big Bad Lake in California. We emerged safe from the accident. But while in the waters, Sri Krishnaji, my husband, co-founder of One World Academy, realized the root cause of all human suffering. He saw what kept suffering alive for days, months and years after a stressful event was over in anyone\'s life. Past and present circumstances, other people and their behavior can trigger stress in us. But they do not keep us chained in our unhappiness. While in the waters, he saw the true reason for humanity\'s suffering. He saw all emotional suffering arising from obsessive, self-centric thinking. It is a total preoccupation with oneself. It is a total incessant preoccupation with oneself. And it is this preoccupation that separates you from the other. It is this preoccupation that is responsible for war, conflict and division of every kind that we see in the world today. This truth can be inconvenient for us. But if we can observe our emotional suffering in the light of this insight, then this truth can liberate us. This truth can set us free. uffering in the light of this insight, then this truth can liberate us. This truth can set us free. In suffering, in unhappiness, in stressful states, you are preoccupied with yourself. We are preoccupied with injustice done to us. We are preoccupied with pleasure that was denied us. We are preoccupied with opportunities that did not come our way. We are preoccupied with love that was withheld from us. It is a total preoccupation with oneself. And in this state, we are not concerned with finding solutions to the problem at hand. For the entire thinking is only me, me, me, me, and me. That is all we think in unhappiness. Let us observe Nomi in the light of this insight. Throughout the story, he is preoccupied. He is preoccupied with his rightness. He is preoccupied with his guilt at having allowed a wrong to happen. He is not concerned about the situation, nor is he concerned about the lady and her son, nor his friend. He is constantly justifying within himself that he was in ignoring the suffering woman. And that he was great in following the rules. Realizing that all lingering in suffering is a total preoccupation with oneself. It is an obsessive, self-centric thinking. It is the most powerful antidote for any form of suffering that could have gripped you. When you see it, not only you walk out of stressful states, but there is a great release of energy. And you awaken to a sense of power, connection, freedom, and purpose. We know the way now. Let\'s do it. It works. Let us do a small three-question meditation. Please close your eyes. Please remember meditation is not about a religion. It is about being human. It is about returning to a Beautiful State. Bring attention to your breath. Inhale and exhale deep and slow. Observe yourself. Are you in a stressful state? Or are you in a Beautiful State? Observe yourself. Are you engaged with your past? Or are you engaged with your future? Or are you in the present? Observe yourself. Are you preoccupied with yourself? Or are you in a state of connection? Observe yourself. Observe yourself. Observe yourself. Are you in a state of connection? Just observe, do not try to change anything. Imagine a flame glowing in the middle of your forehead. Bring a gentle smile to your face. Open your eyes. Look into the eyes of the person sitting next to you. Wish from the bottom of your heart that the other person lives in a Beautiful State. And if you are comfortable, you may hug the other. Thank you so much. Translator: Venki R Reviewer: Queenie Lee Namaste. Let me share a fable with you, for stories are ways of immortalizing messages. Two monks, Yesmi and Nomi, are returning back to the monastery after a day of teaching in the nearby village. They are just about to cross the river when they hear a woman crying. Yesmi walks up to her and asks her what was troubling her? She needed to get back to her toddler who is living in the village across the river. Since the river has risen that day, she is feeling miserable that she will not be able to go back to her toddler, and her child would cry for her all night. On hearing her, Yesmi volunteers to help her. He carries her across the river and drops her on the other side. And they continue with their walk. About half an hour into the walk, Nomi, in a very agitated tone, speaks up, he says, \\"Yesmi, do what you have done?\\" Yesmi calmly looks at him. A master said, \\"Never look at a woman, you spoke to her.\\" A master said, \\"Never speak to a woman, you touched her.\\" A master said, \\"Never touch a woman, you carried her.\\" Yesmi calmly looks at Nomi and says, \\"Yes, that is true, but I have dropped her half an hour ago. It is you who still carries her.\\" Let me take you away from the story for a bit, for a tiny experience of meditation. Please close your eyes. (Music starts: OWA Instrumental - by Enzo Buono) I still see a few sneaking a peek. It is safe to close your eyes. Inhale and exhale, deep and slow. I still see a few sneaking a peek. It is safe to close your eyes. Inhale and exhale, deep and slow. With every exhale raise the edges of your lips into a gentle smile. Inhale. Exhale. Inhale. Exhale. Inhale. Exhale. Broaden your smile. Open your eyes. Do you recognize that with a little attention to your breath and a smile on your face you are more calm and more present. The power to peace is always within you. (Music ends) Let us get back to our story. Yesmi and Nomi represent two states of being. Fundamentally, every one of us lives only in two states. One is a stressful state; the other is a no-stress state. We call this no-stress state a beautiful state. For that is how life feels when you live free of stress. Generally, stress refers to tension, but anger, fear, hurt, loneliness, frustration are all stressful states, aren\'t they? Beautiful state is a state of connection, joy, love, compassion, vitality, and passion. If you are not in a beautiful state, your default state is stress. If you look at the entirety of human life, behind war or peace, cooperation or corrosive politics, love or separation, persistence or failure, finally happy children or a generation that is troubled. Behind all this is the driving force of these two states. Behind every choice and every\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

Human beings today are in a race for survival. In the middle of all of this confusion, how do you encourage people to undergo a personal transformation? Navika Ji, first of all, thank you so much for having me on the show. Individual transformation is at the crux of our work. For collective transformation to happen, we focus a lot on individual transformation. And what does this individual transformation mean? It means that we are teaching people to live in states of great compassion and great oneness, which are enlightened states. So we want people to live in these states so that collectively they can impact humanity\'s consciousness. Our vision for the world is to create 81,000 people in this world, which is 0.001% of the population, who can learn to live in these great states. Great states of compassion and great states of oneness. Now, we as a humanity are connected. We are not separate, but we are connected. And when we, when an individual transforms, that individual transformation is impacting the collective. So today, science speaks about how we are all one field of consciousness., even in the physical world, we are all connected. Matter and energy, they are not separate. They are connected. In the same way, our consciousness is connected to the entire humanity\'s consciousness. So our vision is to bring about this individual transformation where an individual is able to live in these states, in this great enlightened states of great compassion and great oneness. The concept of ACAM or oneness that you are talking about. How does an individual me feel and experience this oneness? We have our work pressures. We have our family pressures. We have our home pressures, friends and a fast moving world around us. How would you make somebody me experience this oneness? How do I go about it? See, if not for oneness, we are talking about a world that is not feeling one. Today, I know I meet so many people and everybody asks me this question. We are not able to feel this connection. We are not able to feel this one. Be it within a family, be it within a work culture, be it between nations,. So if not for this feeling of oneness, we will be experiencing hell if we don\'t feel one. So if we don\'t feel one with our family, if we don\'t feel one with our fellow neighbors, if we don\'t feel one as nations, then what we are experiencing is a lot of separation and a lot of division. Let\'s talk about family. Now, when you come to families, we clearly see that this seeking for feeling one is very, very strong. But yet, that is not happening in families. In today\'s families, most of the time, people are feeling separated. People are feeling disconnected. People are feeling hurt. People are feeling that there is no sense of belonging. They are not experiencing that one. And the result of that is the children who are part of the family are experiencing a tremendous amount of disconnection, a tremendous amount of pressure, stress and anxiety. And we see their lives as they grow up moving into more problems, more problems of disconnection, more problems of not able to feel connected. Loneliness. Loneliness, depression, anxiety, all these are growing. And these are all growing because in the family, there is no sense of oneness. If you come to the human body, for example, human body is a great example. Now, our human body functions in a state of oneness. All the 30 trillion cells are communicating with each other. The enzymes are communicating with each other. 30 trillion cells are communicating with each other. The enzymes are communicating with each other. The hormones are communicating with each other. The organs are communicating with each other. As this communication is happening in a state of oneness, you are in good health. When this communication breaks down, let us say that this communication is not proper and it is not in a state of oneness, then what the human body experiences is sickness. Oneness is the nature of the universe. Oneness is the nature of everything that is existing. Everything is in a flow. Everything is in a harmony. And when a disturbance happens, problems set in. So oneness as a state is very important for individuals and family members. So in the family, when we talk to individuals, what we find is that an individual is unable to feel connected to the family because of his or her anxiety, his or her stress, and more importantly because the person is a broken mirror. So when we talk to an individual, we find out that,, a human being is so many pieces of a broken mirror. And what are these broken mirrors? What is this broken mirror? This broken mirror is different voices. Voice of a mother saying that,, live according to values. Voice of the father, which is saying, go after ambition, become successful no matter what. Voice of the grandparent, which is saying, live a simple life, don\'t be so ambitious. Voice of social media, which is celebrity voice, which is saying,, live them. So there is this constant struggle of trying to be something else from who you are. Constant dissatisfaction with what you have, with who you are, and constantly wanting to get somewhere else. Unless and until all these broken mirrors come together and you heal and you feel one within you, there is no way you can be present to anything in life. You cannot be present to the work you are doing. You cannot be present to your family. And as leaders of nations, you cannot be present to the nation itself. So oneness is that state where you are able to heal, heal your hurts, heal your anger, heal your fear. And you are able to be present and connected to everyone that you are coming in touch with. So how do you do it? So how do you do it? The point is, when we have an active working life, how do we disconnect from all this and begin to do this? How does ACAM help people to do that and get into that form of oneness where we can have different, as we say, different notes of music and make it into a harmonious tune? How do we do that? See, first of all, we should understand that the oneness we are talking about is not an ideal. It\'s not a world where we are seeing everybody agreeing upon the same ideology or same idea. That\'s not the oneness we are talking about. The oneness that we are talking about is the inner state. Now, this inner state, you can get there by practicing simple meditations. You can get there by going through a spiritual journey. We have thousands of people who come to our courses, to our ACAM campus. And they give two weeks in a year. That\'s it for a journey. And they see massive transformation happening in them. Again, it is about healing yourself. It\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

\\u049a\\u04b1\\u0440\\u0430\\u0439 \\u0443\\u043d\\u0440\\u04a3\\u0493\\u0430. \\u042d\\u043a\\u0430\\u043c is a movement in consciousness founded by Sri Sri Preethaji and Sri Sri Krishnaji spread across over a hundred nations of the world. This spiritual movement has led millions to a direct experience of their personal divine and the manifestation of breathtaking miracles. Seekers throng to \\u042d\\u043a\\u0430\\u043c, the mystic powerhouse at the heart of the \\u042d\\u043a\\u0430\\u043c movement to awaken from illusion, ignorance and suffering. Now I have a lot of ease and peace toward myself and I feel that I\'m so complete. and I start love myself and I start connect to the people around me, the nature around me and I start realizing I\'m able to flow with the flow of life. It felt really liberating I finally got through what I was, what was, I don\'t know, what was making me angry or sad at my parents. I finally got through it. And it feels really good. As an entrepreneur, what I have been looking for or what I look for all the time is clarity. And what\'s amazing about Sri Krishnaji is that when you listen to him and you talk to him, he\'s got a complete blueprint of what works and what does not work. And that\'s what I about his program, that they come from a place, a knowledge, a wisdom, that is, does not come from books. It comes from a deep knowing. Aum Asatoma Satgamaya Tamasoma Jyotir Gamaya Vrityorma Mritam Gamaya Every human being has one primal wish that appears as numerous other desires. You want to be happy. But it is sad that you seek this happiness in the outside without ever ridding your own consciousness of suffering, ignorance and separation. Most people are hungry and thirsty children who are lost and abandoned in the middle of a desert. It is this fear and unhappiness within them that makes them hurt each other. But their desert- life is the result of the consciousness with which they live and will disappear upon awakening. An unenlightened consciousness and unawakened consciousness is at the root of most problems. In an unawakened state, you continually engage in generating negative karmic actions and accumulate negative karmic balance which result in obstacles such as relationship conflicts, breakdown of mental and physical health and the lack of uncertainty. It is the result of the world. It is the result of the collective unawakened consciousness that is at the root of most global problems of war, poverty, division and environmental destruction. Your awakening is the only solution\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) must be kept in their original language and not translated.\\n\\n---Data to be Processed---\\n<Entity_types>\\n[Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject]\\n\\n<Input Text>\\n```\\nI n t r o d u c t i o n\\nBy Preethaji\\nAs I open the doors and walk onto the veranda, I feel the crispness of the air turn\\nmoist. The breeze brings the scent of the wet earth from afar. Two large dark\\nclouds gather overhead, unburden themselves, and go their way. Water is owing\\nfrom the roof into puddles in the garden. A frog croaks loudly and another\\nanswers; soon there is an orchestra of them. My senses are exploding with\\ndelight. Bliss is pouring into me from all sides before subsiding into a state of\\ndeep calm. My CFO calls from Los Angeles to discuss our upcoming meditation\\napp. The inner stillness continues through the conversation\\u00a0.\\u00a0.\\u00a0. and the words\\now.\\nWhy does life not always seem so eortless?\\nWhy are deep, rich, and fullling moments in our relationships so few? Why\\nis progress on our climb to achievement so slow and the obstacles so great? Why\\nis happiness so eeting? We feel a ash of joy when we see our children smile,\\nwhen we embrace our loved ones, when we hear applause for a job well done.\\nWhy does our elation fade so quickly, only to be replaced with anxiety, worry, or\\ndoubt?\\nFor thousands of years, human beings have sought answers to these\\nquestions. We have pursued countless strategies for awakening to a more\\nbeautiful existence. We have worked hard on honing our skills, mastering\\nancient disciplines and trendy hacks alike.\\nBut has all this strategizing brought us any closer to the lives we dream of? Or\\nis it just yielding temporary results?\\nTo be sure, there is nothing wrong with a tactical approach. But our goal is to\\nhelp you go beyond the mediocre and awaken to a power that is much greater\\nthan any technique you can master\\u2014a power that will enable you to create a life\\nof great prosperity and great love. You need only unlock it.\\nIt is the power of a transformed consciousness.\\nTo put it another way, we\\u2019re not talking about retraining the mind or\\nembracing better habits; we\\u2019re talking about nothing less than transforming the\\nway you experience reality. The way you experience yourself. The world around\\nyou. Everything.\\nThink about that for a moment.\\nWhat would it mean to experience life in a completely dierent way? To feel\\nas if new parts of your brain have been activated and revved up? To see\\nopportunity where you only saw obstacles? To sense that nally time and\\nfortune are on your side?\\nWhat would be possible with such a powerful consciousness?\\nIf you are like many of the people we\\u2019ve met in the past three decades, you are\\nhungry for such knowledge. Thirty years ago, my in-laws, Sri Bhagavan and\\nAmma, founded Oneness, a spiritual organization dedicated to helping\\nindividuals move from survival to living, and twenty years later, my husband,\\nKrishnaji, and I founded One World Academy, our own philosophy and\\nmeditation school for transforming consciousness.\\nIt has been two years since my in-laws handed over Oneness to Krishnaji and\\nme, and we have since then merged these two great organizations to create O&O\\nAcademy. Our curriculum has helped thousands of people nurture relationships\\nfree from hurt, create achievements free from aggression, and live free of fear. It\\nhas taught them how to move from separation to connection, division to\\noneness, stress to calm\\u2014and, in so doing, transform themselves as individuals\\nand as members of families and organizations.\\nO&O Academy is not an ashram for settlers, but rather an organization that\\nengages its students in dynamic learning. You take what you learn here and you\\napply it to everyday life. We oer courses for teens, young adults, families,\\nspiritual seekers, conscious wealth creators, and leaders who want a\\n\\u201cconsciousness upgrade.\\u201d\\nMany initially come to Krishnaji and me for exactly the kind of strategic\\nadvice I just wrote about, but it doesn\\u2019t take them long to realize that all\\nstrategies pale in comparison with the insights and the magic that unfold in their\\nlives as a result of awakening to the sheer power of consciousness.\\nThink of this book as a guide to unlocking the awesome potential of human\\nconsciousness. Tragically, most of us have never been taught to tap into this deep\\nsource of wisdom. No wonder we spend so much time plotting and planning for\\nhappiness or achievement as if they were guests who can never be convinced to\\nstay for a cup of tea!\\nIn the pages that follow, we will share with you four sacred secrets, which will\\nattune you to the great power of consciousness. Each secret is followed by a life\\njourney designed to set you free of all that is preventing you from realizing your\\ndreams, from accessing expansive states of consciousness, and from truly\\nconnecting with your loved ones.\\nThe great reward of a transformed consciousness is that you will come to\\nexperience a way of being in the world that Krishnaji and I call a \\u201cbeautiful\\nstate.\\u201d From such a state, life feels joyful and eortless. Opportunities start to\\now into your life with ease. Random strangers become friends and supporters.\\nHelp comes to you at every step of the journey. No more do you nd yourself\\nfeeling stuck. Your intuition is awake.\\nThe insight at the heart of this book is simple enough: there are only two\\nstates of being, a suering state and a beautiful state. One state creates an energy\\neld of chaos around you. The other welcomes harmonious happening into\\nyour life. Therefore, the most important choice any of us can ever make is \\u201cIn\\nwhich state do I want to live?\\u201d\\nBut this idea also presents a question: Once we make the choice, can we walk\\naround in a beautiful state all the time?\\nNo, we cannot. A choice alone is not enough. We must rst understand that\\nsuering states are often unconscious and deeply ingrained. They took root in us\\neither epigenetically, prenatally, in our early childhoods, or even in our later\\nyears.\\nOur suering states prevent us from feeling this sense of wholeness, calm, joy,\\nand courage. But they can be overcome.\\nIf we don\\u2019t learn to free ourselves from suering states, they\\u2019ll come back\\ntime and time again until our baseline mood is sadness, irritation, or anger. From\\nsuch wounded places, we cannot create lasting happiness, relationships, or\\nwealth. Even if we use our frustration to drive us forward, our accomplishments\\nbring us little lasting satisfaction. Or, even worse, our rise to the top comes at\\nsuch a steep price that we nd ourselves asking: Was it even worth it?\\nAt that point, meditation, chanting, or vacations can feel like tossing ice\\ncubes into a volcano.\\nWe need more than balms; we need transformation.\\nWe have coauthored this book to share our own experience of the power of\\nthe beautiful state as well as the experiences of students of ours who have\\ntransformed their lives from the inside out in every aspect, from building lasting\\nrelationships to creating\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) must be kept in their original language and not translated.\\n\\n---Data to be Processed---\\n<Entity_types>\\n[Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject]\\n\\n<Input Text>\\n```\\nMy Awakening\\nBy Krishnaji\\nPreethaji and I had just traveled to Big Bear Lake in Southern California with\\nour daughter, Lokaa, who was ve at the time. It was the spring of 2009. We\\nwere on a vacation we had been looking forward to for some time, and we stood\\ntogether at the top of the mountain taking in the beauty around us.\\nThe vast lake of crystal-blue water seemed to have no end. Pockets of green\\nand white reected back the earth and the sky. Streaks of liquid silver and gold\\npierced the pristine surface. I felt exhilarated by the crisp, cool earthiness that\\nlled my lungs: we had expected a change in temperature so high up in the\\nmountains, but none of us imagined that the breeze o the snow-fed lake would\\nbe so biting. My body and mind were totally awake.\\nAfter a few moments, Lokaa\\u2019s excitement broke the silence. \\u201cNanna, Nanna,\\nlook!\\u201d she cried, using the South Indian endearment for \\u201cDad.\\u201d Tugging on my\\narm, she pointed toward the marina, where two Jet Skis were pulling into the\\ndock. Preethaji and I looked at each other. How could we say no to such\\nenthusiasm?\\nLokaa\\u2019s excitement was truly contagious. The ski instructor, too, had a jovial\\nair about him. After he had run through the basics, he queried, \\u201cYou guys really\\nwant life jackets?\\u201d\\nHe asked it so casually, I almost immediately said, \\u201cNo, we\\u2019ll be ne.\\u201d Maybe\\nthirty seconds passed before Preethaji nudged me and said, \\u201cLet\\u2019s take them.\\u201d In\\na moment it dawned on me: of course we needed them! Preethaji can\\u2019t swim. We\\ngrabbed the vests, then made our way to our Jet Ski.\\nAs I red the ignition, the instructor struggled to convey the nal guidelines\\nover the noise of the engine and Lokaa\\u2019s cheers. He shouted a few details about\\nwatching our speed and avoiding sharp turns. Then, just as we started to pull\\naway, he yelled, \\u201cIf you ip, get it upright in seven minutes or else it will sink.\\u201d\\nWith that, o we went.\\n\\u201cFaster, Nanna, faster,\\u201d Lokaa egged me on over our laughter. We had already\\ncovered quite a distance, yet it felt like we could keep going for miles on the lake.\\nI wanted to give both Lokaa and Preethaji a memorable experience, so I\\ncranked things up a notch. I began to weave back and forth, hoping to create a\\nnice, big wave. Instead, the Jet Ski toppled over and we fell out.\\nEverything turned black. We were all underwater. Fear tore through my body\\nas I felt Preethaji desperately pulling on my clothes. Where was Lokaa? Flailing, I\\nmade my way to the surface and watched as both of them popped up with their\\nlife jackets securely buckled.\\nPreethaji had inhaled water into her lungs and was gasping for breath. As she\\nstruggled to nd balance, my mind swung wildly. What if something had\\nhappened to her? To Lokaa? A few minutes passed before I could steady and\\ncomfort them. Lokaa recovered faster than Preethaji.\\n\\u201cKanna? How do we turn this thing over?\\u201d Preethaji shouted.\\nThe instructor\\u2019s words echoed loudly in my ears as the tension grew. We were\\ngetting close to the seven-minute mark, so surely the Jet Ski would begin to sink\\nany moment now.\\nWe were stuck way oshore in icy water with soaked phones. It wasn\\u2019t too\\nmuch of a leap to think that someone who\\u2019d been so casual about safety\\nprocedures might have forgotten us. What if no one comes to help us? I thought,\\nin a panic. We would freeze in these frigid waters. While I was unable to manage\\nto turn the Jet Ski upright, thankfully it was still oating. We would still need to\\nwait for someone to rescue us, but for the moment it seemed the worst danger\\nhad passed.\\nMeanwhile, my mind continued to race. I couldn\\u2019t stop raging about the\\npoor instructions we were given at the marina; I wanted to scold the Jet Ski\\noperator\\u2014I was so angry. At the same time, I struggled to understand why this\\nhad happened in the rst place. Questions rushed through my head chaotically.\\nWhy did this happen to my family? Was it the result of negative karma? Was\\nit destined to happen as part of some cosmic plan?\\nWhat lesson am I supposed to learn from this?\\nNone of the answers I came up with did anything to make me feel better. If I\\ncould chalk the accident up to karma, a cosmic plan, or some lesson I needed to\\nlearn, then surely knowing that would dissolve my anger and bring me some\\npeace, and my questions should cease. Instead, my anger and my questions\\ncontinued unabated.\\nWhat exactly is going on? What is this su\\ufb00ering I am feeling within?\\nI have always felt comfortable asking big questions like this. In fact, you\\nmight say I was raised to do just that. My father, Sri Bhagavan, is a spiritual\\nteacher and founder of Oneness, a spiritual organization. At the heart of the\\nmovement is the phenomenon of deeksha, the Oneness blessing. When my\\nfather was still a child, a mystic vision of a gigantic golden orb of light would\\nappear to him, provoking him to chant and meditate for the liberation of\\nhumankind. He went on to found a school, where along with conventional\\neducation children also learned the art of joyful relationships. I was a student\\nthere, too.\\nFifteen years after my father\\u2019s visions ceased, they spontaneously began in me.\\nI was eleven years old when I began to experience states of consciousness that\\nwere like none I\\u2019d ever heard of or experienced before. And these states suddenly\\nbegan to ow from me to my friends and fellow students.\\nWhen my dad asked me one day if I could consciously share the experience I\\nhad with others, I said yes. When I transferred the state to others, they began to\\nsee the same vision of the golden orb of light.\\nSome called it God; some called it love; some called it sacred.\\nBecause of my unique childhood, I have never shied away from investigating\\nthe mysteries of life. And yet, never had philosophical questions taken on such\\nurgency.\\nUnfortunately, none of the explanations I grasped at did anything to make\\nme feel better as I lay stranded in the cold waters. None helped me nd calm. I\\nfelt the blood rush to my face as I thought of the useless instructor. He never\\ntold us how to right the ski if we ipped. How could he have forgotten such an\\nimportant piece of information? How careless could someone be?\\nI just could not let go of my anger. My thinking was going in circles. This was\\nstrange to me, because ever since my childhood, I have never let disturbance take\\nroot in me.\\nExtremely uncomfortable with my inner\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

In addition, we also have\\nfounded two enormous charities that have thus far impacted the lives of more\\nthan 500,000 villagers in more than one thousand villages around our academy.\\nWe have benecially aected the lives of the more than 220,000 young people in\\nvarious schools and colleges in India who have gone through our courses. We\\nalso have founded ve global businesses for which we have been acting as\\nvisionaries and guides in recent years.\\nIt is not an exaggeration to tell you that we are deeply fullled and successful\\nin all we do. If you want to nd\\nsolutions to your problems, if you want your desires to be fullled, you must\\nawaken to the power of your own consciousness.\\nWhat we are about to share with you will make your consciousness powerful\\nenough to achieve your heartfelt intentions. These four sacred secrets are drawn\\nfrom our lives and have worked in the lives of every person we have taught them\\nto.\\nSo open your hearts. Even as you read each of these sacred secrets and absorb\\nthem, you will see the ow of your life shifting toward the miraculous.\\nDiscover the rst sacred secret.\\nThe First Sacred Secret: Live with a Spiritual\\nVision Which state of being is carrying you?\\nBy Preethaji\\nThis ancient fable will lead you into comprehending the rst sacred secret. Read\\nit slowly.\\nTwo monks, Yesmi and Nomi, were returning to their monastery after a day\\nof teaching in the nearby village. On their way back, they had to cross a river.\\nJust as they were about to enter the river, they heard a woman crying.\\nYesmi approached the woman and inquired what was troubling her.\\n\\u201cI need to get back to my toddler, who is waiting for me in the village across\\nthe river. Since the waters have risen today, I am unable to get back,\\u201d she said.\\nShe was miserable that her child would cry for her all night.\\nYesmi volunteered to get her across the river. After he carried her to the other\\nside, she thanked him, and the two monks continued on their way to the\\nmonastery.\\nAfter a long, uncomfortable silence, Nomi nally spoke. In an agitated tone\\nhe said, \\u201cDo you realize the seriousness of what you just did?\\u201d\\nYesmi smiled. \\u201cI know.\\u201d\\nNomi continued, \\u201cOur master said, \\u2018Never look at a woman,\\u2019 and you spoke\\nto her! Master said, \\u2018Never speak to a woman,\\u2019 and you touched her! Master said,\\n\\u2018Never touch a woman,\\u2019 and you carried her!\\u201d\\nYesmi calmly replied, \\u201cThis is true, but I set her down half an hour ago. Is it\\nnot you who is still carrying her?\\u201d\\nThe two monks in the story represent the two inner states that all living\\nbeings experience. At every moment in our lives, we are either living in a\\nsuering state or a no-suering state.\\nLet us call this no-suering state a \\u201cbeautiful state,\\u201d because that is how life\\nfeels when we are not suering.\\nIf you\\u2019re uncomfortable with the word \\u201csuering,\\u201d replace it with the word\\n\\u201cstress.\\u201d Generally, stress refers to tension, but anger, fear, loneliness, frustration\\nare all stressful states, aren\\u2019t they? The word \\u201csuering\\u201d embraces all these states.\\nBeautiful states include experiences of calm, connection, passion, joy, vitality,\\nand inner peace. When we are not in a beautiful state, our default state is stress\\nor suering.\\nIf we look at every single happening in our own lives or the world around us,\\nwe see the driving force of these two states of being. Behind war or peace,\\naddictions or social harmony, persistence or failure, kindness or cruelty,\\ncooperation or corrosive politics, and, nally, happy children or a generation\\nthat is troubled, there is either a suering state or a beautiful state.\\nLet us now return to our story, keeping in mind that Yesmi represents a\\nbeautiful state and Nomi a stressful or suering state.\\nNomi created a nonexistent problem in his mind and got stressed trying to\\nsolve it. Yesmi solved a fellow human being\\u2019s actual problem and continued on\\nhis peaceful walk.\\nNomi was agitated before the event, during the event, and after the event. His\\ninner agitation led him to overcomplicate matters and act irrationally.\\nYesmi, while in action, was fully present. Once the action was over, his\\ndeparture from the situation, too, was total. In a beautiful state, there is no\\ncompulsive rumination over the past or anxiety about the future. We experience\\ninner simplicity and the brilliance of an uncluttered mind. We are connected to\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

Read\\nit slowly.\\nTwo monks, Yesmi and Nomi, were returning to their monastery after a day\\nof teaching in the nearby village. On their way back, they had to cross a river.\\nJust as they were about to enter the river, they heard a woman crying.\\nYesmi approached the woman and inquired what was troubling her.\\n\\u201cI need to get back to my toddler, who is waiting for me in the village across\\nthe river. Since the waters have risen today, I am unable to get back,\\u201d she said.\\nShe was miserable that her child would cry for her all night.\\nYesmi volunteered to get her across the river. After he carried her to the other\\nside, she thanked him, and the two monks continued on their way to the\\nmonastery.\\nAfter a long, uncomfortable silence, Nomi nally spoke. In an agitated tone\\nhe said, \\u201cDo you realize the seriousness of what you just did?\\u201d\\nYesmi smiled. \\u201cI know.\\u201d\\nNomi continued, \\u201cOur master said, \\u2018Never look at a woman,\\u2019 and you spoke\\nto her! Master said, \\u2018Never speak to a woman,\\u2019 and you touched her! Master said,\\n\\u2018Never touch a woman,\\u2019 and you carried her!\\u201d\\nYesmi calmly replied, \\u201cThis is true, but I set her down half an hour ago. Is it\\nnot you who is still carrying her?\\u201d\\nThe two monks in the story represent the two inner states that all living\\nbeings experience. At every moment in our lives, we are either living in a\\nsuering state or a no-suering state.\\nLet us call this no-suering state a \\u201cbeautiful state,\\u201d because that is how life\\nfeels when we are not suering.\\nIf you\\u2019re uncomfortable with the word \\u201csuering,\\u201d replace it with the word\\n\\u201cstress.\\u201d Generally, stress refers to tension, but anger, fear, loneliness, frustration\\nare all stressful states, aren\\u2019t they? The word \\u201csuering\\u201d embraces all these states.\\nBeautiful states include experiences of calm, connection, passion, joy, vitality,\\nand inner peace. When we are not in a beautiful state, our default state is stress\\nor suering.\\nIf we look at every single happening in our own lives or the world around us,\\nwe see the driving force of these two states of being. Behind war or peace,\\naddictions or social harmony, persistence or failure, kindness or cruelty,\\ncooperation or corrosive politics, and, nally, happy children or a generation\\nthat is troubled, there is either a suering state or a beautiful state.\\nLet us now return to our story, keeping in mind that Yesmi represents a\\nbeautiful state and Nomi a stressful or suering state.\\nNomi created a nonexistent problem in his mind and got stressed trying to\\nsolve it. Yesmi solved a fellow human being\\u2019s actual problem and continued on\\nhis peaceful walk.\\nNomi was agitated before the event, during the event, and after the event. His\\ninner agitation led him to overcomplicate matters and act irrationally.\\nYesmi, while in action, was fully present. Once the action was over, his\\ndeparture from the situation, too, was total. In a beautiful state, there is no\\ncompulsive rumination over the past or anxiety about the future. We experience\\ninner simplicity and the brilliance of an uncluttered mind. We are connected to\\nthe present.\\nNomi was lost because a stressful state separates. When we are Nomi, we\\nstand in a joyful crowd and still feel absent. We feel lonely even when we are with\\nclose friends.\\nYesmi\\u2019s state was dierent; he was present. He also felt Nomi\\u2019s distress and\\ntried to help him out of it with a wise question.\\nWhen we are in a beautiful state, we are wise enough to help ourselves and to\\nhelp others. Our actions are decisive and powerful.\\nThere have been times when we have all been Yesmi, and there are times when\\nwe have been Nomi. We have all lived in stress and disconnection and have\\ncontributed to the chaos in our personal lives and the lives of the people around\\nus. We have also lived in beautiful states of connection and have contributed to\\nthe well-being of the world and ourselves.\\nIn our years of observation of consciousness and its manifestation in life, we\\nhave noticed a recurring pattern. Undoubtedly, suering is destructive and\\nbeautiful states are rejuvenating and life-giving. Time and again, we have noticed\\nthat the more a person lives in a suering state, the more life becomes like a\\ntangled net from which there seems to be no escape. Problems mount,\\nconfusion mounts, chaos mounts. Life becomes an endless battle.\\nWhen we prolong suering states of frustration, disappointment, jealousy, or\\nhate, every aspect of our lives feels o-kilter. We battle with our families, battle at\\nwork, battle with the government. In suering states, we feel as if all the forces in\\nthe universe are hostile to us. No matter what decision we make or what action\\nwe take, we nd life heading toward greater and greater chaos.\\nWe have also seen, on innumerable occasions, that when we live in a beautiful\\nstate, magical \\u201csynchronicities\\u201d begin to unfold. Here, you might be wondering,\\nwhat is a \\u201csynchronicity\\u201d? Synchronicities are meaningful coincidences. They\\nare favorable and harmonious occurrences that happen in alignment with your\\nintention. It feels as if the randomly moving universe is arranging itself in\\npatterns in response to your heart\\u2019s longing to support you.\\nIn a beautiful state we become more creative, and incredible solutions to\\nchallenges arise. Our damaged relationships heal and nourishing new\\nrelationships emerge. Our thinking becomes clearer, our intellects become\\nsharper, our minds become more peaceful, and our hearts move into a space of\\nconnection.\\nIf you are feeling overwhelmed by the concept of a beautiful state or are\\nunsure if you have understood it correctly, remember that a beautiful state refers\\nto a rich range of experiences. It can begin as serenity, happiness, gratitude, love,\\nor courage. The essence of a beautiful state is the absence of conicting inner\\nchatter, a greater presence to life, and a richer connection to the people around\\nyou. As you evolve further, you can awaken to transcendental states such as\\npeace, stillness, compassion, joy, and fearlessness. In these states you are in a ow\\nwith life. You awaken to the oneness and interconnection of all existence. The\\nmore powerful the state, the more easily you impact the fabric of consciousness\\nto manifest your aspirations.\\nUnpacking the Word\\nBy Krishnaji\\nTo live totally we must end the fear of death.\\nTo love completely we must dissolve disappointment.\\nTo experience a beautiful state we must have a penetrating insight into\\nsuering and thus become free of it.\\nBy now you have likely come to understand the way we use the word\\n\\u201csuering.\\u201d Simply\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

They called it \\u201cBrahma Garbha\\u201d\\u2014the\\nwomb of limitless consciousness\\u2014and they associated it with the pineal, the\\npituitary, and the hypothalamus axes in the brain.\\nIt has been our experience that when a person activates this part of their\\nconsciousness with a practice like Soul Sync, their heartfelt intentions become\\npowerful enough to break through the thought barrier and enter the world of\\nmatter. Afterward you feel as if you\\u2019ve established a new personal relationship\\nwith the universe: it seems to be rearranging itself in such a way that you begin\\nto experience synchronicities. Life takes miraculous turns and heads toward a\\nmagnicent destiny.\\nWhether your goal is nancial security, a nurturing relationship, a\\nmeaningful career, a deeper spiritual life, or a connection with the universe, you\\ncan use this practice as your go-to place to create magic.\\nHere are the steps for Soul Sync.\\nThe Great Soul Sync\\nThe P ostur e\\nSit in a comfortable chair or on a meditation cushion. Rest your palms on your\\nthighs and use your thumbs to count out breaths on your \\ufb01ngers. Start with the\\nindex \\ufb01nger of your left hand, then your middle \\ufb01nger, and so on until you reach a\\ncount of eight. If you\\u2019re meditating with a child, you can shorten the count to four.\\nHow It W orks\\nWhen we practice Soul Sync, we silence the chemical activity that triggers con\\ufb02ict\\nso that we can move into a beautiful state of relaxation and calm.\\n\\u2022\\u00a0STEP ONE. Start by taking eight deep inhalations and slow exhalations. As you\\nmove from one breath to the next, keep count with your \\ufb01ngers. It\\u2019s natural for\\nyour attention to wander frequently. Simply bring it back and continue the count\\nfrom where you lost your attention. By the time you \\ufb01nish this \\ufb01rst stage, your\\nparasympathetic nervous system will be fully active. This kind of breathing\\nactivates your long, winding vagus nerve, which exits the brain and interfaces\\nwith the heart, lungs, and digestive tract. Activating your vagus nerve causes\\nyour entire autonomic nervous system to basically chill out.\\nYour pulse will begin to slow down, and your blood pressure will be more\\nbalanced. Even your digestive system will react positively. According to Dr.\\nAndrew Newberg and Mark Robert Waldman, this conscious repetitive\\nmovement of your hands further enhances the motor and coordination centers\\nof your brain, thereby increasing e\\ufb03ciency throughout the brain. It facilitates\\nmemory formation and memory retrieval.\\n\\u2022\\u00a0STEP TWO. Inhale deeply and, while exhaling, make the humming sound of a\\nbee at a base pitch. Making the hum as long as you comfortably can and\\nlistening to the sound with total attention deepens your relaxation. Do not\\nstretch your exhalation to the point of straining. Once again, repeat for eight full\\nbreaths. This part of your Soul Sync improves your sleep quality and calms your\\nblood pressure.\\n\\u2022\\u00a0STEP THREE. Observe the pause between inhaling and exhaling for eight cycles\\nof breath. When we inhale and exhale, a natural pause occurs after each\\ninhalation, right before exhalation begins. Observe the pause. This might be a\\nbit tricky. However, once you begin to perceive this pause, you will experience a\\nslowing down of your thoughts. Do not try to force a pause, hold your breath, or\\nexaggerate it. Your breath should be natural and smooth.\\n\\u2022\\u00a0STEP FOUR. Take your meditation beyond calm to expansion. For the next eight\\nbreaths, inhale and exhale as you inwardly chant \\u201cAh-hum,\\u201d which means \\u201cI am\\u201d\\nor \\u201cI am limitless consciousness\\u201d in the ancient language of Sanskrit.\\n\\u2022\\u00a0STEP FIVE. Imagine or feel your body transforming into light. Imagine the \\ufb02oor,\\nthe table, the people around you\\u2014everything\\u2014expanding into one unitary \\ufb01eld of\\nenergy. In this \\ufb01eld of consciousness, everything is connected. There are no\\nseparate objects, no separate people, and no separate events. You; every person\\nyou have ever met or known; every species of plant or animal that has ever\\nexisted; all your hopes and aspirations; everything you have ever seen, felt,\\nheard, or known; everything you have ever thought or conceived\\u2014all exist as one\\nunitary \\ufb01eld of consciousness. There is no separation or division. In this \\ufb01eld,\\nthought and matter are one. Desire and reality are one.\\n\\u2022\\u00a0STEP SIX. After you have immersed yourself in that endless expanse of light,\\nbegin to feel or imagine your heartfelt desire as if it were happening in the now.\\nImagine, for example, that you have the desire to heal your relationship with a\\nloved one. In this stage, feel and imagine the joy that a transformed relationship\\nwould give both of you. Or if you\\u2019re dreaming of starting a new career, see and\\nfeel yourself in your role. Experience the state you would be in when you would\\nactually live your dream. Stay in that space for a few moments. Open your eyes\\nand move on when you\\u2019re ready.\\nThe Best Time of Da y to Soul Sync\\nMany people practice Soul Sync when they \\ufb01rst wake up, but you can do it anytime.\\nSome practice before they make an important decision. Others do it to wind down\\nand de-stress at the end of a tiring day or whenever they \\ufb01nd themselves getting\\ncaught up in an agitated state of mind.\\nYou can practice alone or in a group. Some organizations practice it in the\\nmorning before they begin their day to calm themselves. There are teams that use\\nSoul Sync to set a common vision and harness their collective power to achieve it.\\nWe suggest setting the goal of practicing it at least once every day, but don\\u2019t limit\\nyourself. Some people practice it as many as \\ufb01ve times a day. We also suggest\\nthat you never rush the practice. It takes only about nine minutes, but it will leave\\nyou feeling magical your entire day.\\nOne of our graduates, an entrepreneur who just launched a new start-up, has\\nmade Soul Sync a daily practice for her entire team.\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) must be kept in their original language and not translated.\\n\\n---Data to be Processed---\\n<Entity_types>\\n[Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject]\\n\\n<Input Text>\\n```\\nThe First Life Journey: Heal the\\nWounded Child\\nBy Krishnaji\\nMost people live with a sense of self-imposed claustrophobia.\\nPerhaps you have experienced this painful state yourself. It\\u2019s as if a crowd\\nshowed up at your front door and announced they were throwing a party. Only\\nthis was not just any crowd: the crew ooding into your living room uninvited\\nincluded everyone who had ever wronged you, hurt you, made you feel ashamed\\nto be yourself.\\nBefore you knew it, they began to oer unsolicited opinions about the decor,\\nthe choice of music, and, well, every decision you\\u2019ve ever made! They were loud,\\nthey were judgmental, and they didn\\u2019t want to leave.\\nYou did anything to escape the cacophony of criticism. But it was impossible\\nto ignore this crowd and, unfortunately, wine only made them louder!\\nThe more you asked them to leave, the noisier they became. Not knowing\\nwhat to do, you froze. Sure, you would have loved nothing more than some\\npeace and quiet. And yet, strangely enough, after a few hours of this racket, you\\ngot used to your unwelcome guests\\u2014ma ny of them were, after all, the people\\nyou loved most: your parents, your siblings, your rst friends.\\nStill, the longer they stayed, the more they wore you down. You found it\\nbecame impossible to disentangle their words, opinions, and ideas from your\\nown. You started to feel tentative, as skittish as a cat on a fence. If only you could\\nnd a little space.\\nIf only you could get unstuck.\\nIf only you could start moving forward again.\\nLife is a great river: ever rushing forward, always presenting us with new\\nopportunities for love, connection, and expansion. But if we want to move\\nforward with it, we must become free of our past that tethers us to the murky\\nand stagnant banks where no forward motion is possible.\\nTo return to our party analogy, we must make peace with those unwanted\\nguests who have taken up residence in our minds and hearts. We must awaken to\\nthe dimension of calm in our consciousness. We must arise to a state of total\\npresence to all the voices telling us that we are stupid, foolish, unworthy\\u2014as well\\nas the ones telling us that we are right and everyone else is wrong.\\nHow?\\nBy healing the wounded child that lives within us all, frozen in time, its cries\\ndrowned out by the raucous crowd. We must hold the spiritual vision to loosen\\nthe grip of the past so that our inner ice may thaw and we break free from the\\nrigidity of the past. So we can be present to the present and move eortlessly into\\nthe future.\\nWhen we do, there is no turning back anymore. Our life will begin to ow\\nlike a great river toward the ocean\\u2014to ward greater order, well-being, and\\nexpansion.\\nLet\\u2019s begin.\\nImagine that while waiting in line at a trendy restaurant, you trip and fall. A\\nhush falls over the diners and your cheeks redden with shame. You tried so hard\\nto look fashionable, and with one wrong step the truth was revealed to the\\nworld:\\nYou don\\u2019t belong here and everyone knows it.\\nLong after you dust yourself o, you obsess about the fall. The physical pain\\nwas minimal, but the emotional upset lingers long after you lost your balance.\\nAnd when life beckons you with the next experience, you are too lost in a whirl\\nof chaotic thinking. You are drowning in the din of your own inner conict.\\nNow imagine yourself instead as a happy toddler taking your rst steps.\\nWhen you fall down and hurt your knee, you cry. But as soon as the physical\\npain fades, something else catches your eye. You are ready for the next experience\\neven before the tears have dried. It is as if the pain never happened.\\nSuch is the beautiful state of joy of a happy child. Just as birds leave no trace\\nof their paths in the sky, our past leaves behind no painful emotional trail. It is a\\nclear slate of consciousness that is ready for the next experience.\\nThe happy child and the wounded child are not mere memories of our past.\\nThey are beautiful and suering states of being we still experience, whether we\\nare aware of them or not.\\nAll of us have been a happy child at some point in our lives. We have all\\nexperienced a state that was free from fear and unhappiness. As a happy child\\nyou are not afraid of making mistakes. You are not stuck in a whirl of self-centric\\nmisery. You smile more radiantly, you laugh joyfully, you cry freely, and you love\\ndeeply. Life feels uncomplicated. There emerges a calm conviction about\\ncreating a beautiful destiny\\u2014one that does not require constant repetition and\\narmation on your part. There is no longer a half-hearted or casual approach to\\nwork or relationships.\\nThis happy child is refreshingly innocent\\u2014and joyfully honest!\\nThere is a popular YouTube video with over 114 million views and counting\\n\\u2014https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E8aprCNnecU\\u2014of a young boy and\\nhis mother having a conversation about love\\u2014and cookies. The boy tells his\\nmother that while he loves her, he does not always like her.\\nHe only likes her when she gives him cookies!\\nEven if we were too young to remember, we have all been that happy child.\\nWe have all existed in that simple state: we like the things we believe cause us\\nhappiness, and we dislike the things we believe cause us pain. For in the beautiful\\nstate of the happy child, it doesn\\u2019t matter if feelings are \\u201cright\\u201d or \\u201cwrong\\u201d: they\\nare our own. They are real to us. Because we have not yet learned to judge\\nourselves for having such feelings, we are happy.\\nWhat, then, causes the disappearance of this happy child state? How does the\\nwounded child state appear in its place?\\nWell, we all know what happens after the happy child gives some honest\\nappraisal of the world. The adults chuckle at the innocent audacity, and a well-\\nmeaning parent, uncle, or aunt tells the child, \\u201cThis is not how a good boy\\nbehaves. A good boy always loves his parents\\u00a0.\\u00a0.\\u00a0. just like he loves eating his\\nvegetables and doing his homework.\\u201d\\nDespite the good intentions behind them, such statements can plant seeds of\\ndoubt, confusion, and even shame in the child\\u2019s mind. His inner experiences\\nmay not have changed: he still likes his parents best when he is being fed things\\nhe likes; he still feels jealous of the child with the best toys; he still nds certain\\nkinds of schoolwork boring.\\nBut now he feels ashamed of those feelings.\\nTime passes. The child grows up, but often with a great deal of inner conict.\\nWe often chalk that dissatisfaction up to the natural process of adulthood.\\nBut what if this suering state of being is actually quite unnatural? What if\\nthere is a way to return to that beautiful state of joy?\\nWhat Is Your True Nature\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

["langchain", "schema", "messages", "HumanMessage"], "kwargs": {"content": "---Task---\\nExtract entities and relationships from the input text in Data to be Processed below.\\n\\n---Instructions---\\n1.  **Strict Adherence to Format:** Strictly adhere to all format requirements for entity and relationship lists, including output order, field delimiters, and proper noun handling, as specified in the system prompt.\\n2.  **Output Content Only:** Output *only* the extracted list of entities and relationships. Do not include any introductory or concluding remarks, explanations, or additional text before or after the list.\\n3.  **Completion Signal:** Output `<|COMPLETE|>` as the final line after all relevant entities and relationships have been extracted and presented.\\n4.  **Output Language:** Ensure the output language is English. Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) must be kept in their original language and not translated.\\n\\n---Data to be Processed---\\n<Entity_types>\\n[Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject]\\n\\n<Input Text>\\n```\\nThe Second Sacred Secret: Discover\\nYour Inner Truth\\nBy Preethaji\\nEvery one of us aspires to greatness\\u2014to  be a great parent, a great partner, a great\\nprofessional, a great athlete, a great wealth creator, or a great change-maker. But\\nit is my deep conviction that a total owering of our consciousness must occur\\nbefore the universe begins to manifest any form of greatness through us. And\\nthis authentic transformation is only possible when we live our lives in consort\\nwith inner truth.\\nWithout inner truth all spiritual growth is merely the pursuit of a beautiful\\nideal; it is empty poetry lost in words without a radiant essence sustaining it.\\nLet us explore this secret by reecting upon the story of the father of the\\nIndian nation, Gandhi, and the actual moment of his transformation from\\nGandhi to Mahatma Gandhi. Mahatma, which means \\u201cthe Great Soul,\\u201d is how\\nIndia sees the man who has been one of the key inuencers in human history, an\\nemblem of the weak\\u2019s victory over their oppressors through the path of\\nnonviolence.\\nIn 1893, Gandhi moved to South Africa to try his luck at becoming\\nsuccessful as a young lawyer. Shortly after his arrival, Gandhi had to travel from\\nDurban to Pretoria for a court case. He had purchased a rst-class ticket by mail.\\nWhen the white ticket collector insulted him by referring to him as a \\u201ccolored\\nman\\u201d and a \\u201ccoolie\\u201d and ordered him to move to the third-class compartment\\nwith his belongings, Gandhi stubbornly refused on the grounds of possessing a\\nvalid ticket. The ticket collector stopped the train and unceremoniously forced\\nGandhi o the compartment onto the cold platform of the tiny railway station\\nin Pietermartizburg.\\nLet us now move away from the facts of history and explore Gandhi\\u2019s inner\\nstate as he sat shivering in the freezing cold with indignity. What you are about\\nto read is how Krishnaji and I interpret what happened to Gandhi in his most\\ncrucial rst experience of transformation.\\nHere were some options that were open to Gandhi, who was simmering in\\nhumiliation. Option one was to ditch the entire plan of succeeding as a lawyer in\\nSouth Africa and return to India, bristling with anger. Option two was to\\nstomach the shame and go about his eorts at moneymaking, as numerous\\nothers had done before him. Option three was to burn with indignation, stay in\\nSouth Africa, and plan his personal revenge on the ticket collector or rouse an\\nangry rebellion against the British Empire.\\nGandhi chose the fourth option: bringing his attention to his suering states\\nof anger and shame and dissolving them. And from that state of calm he could\\nmove beyond himself and connect to scores of Indians who suered oppression\\nday after day.\\nIt was not his personal hate for the British but a deep compassion for his\\npeople that led him to begin his nonviolent resistance movement to injustice in\\nSouth Africa. Fifty-four years after his rst major brush with inner truth,\\nMahatma Gandhi led a nation of more than 390 million Indians to\\nindependence from the British without inciting them to violence and\\nbloodshed. It was a ght led from a beautiful state.\\nEquipped with this story, let us now take a plunge into the second sacred\\nsecret.\\nSo many in the world think that to rise to greatness, we must put our\\nstrategies and plans in place, know the plans of our opponents, and get the\\nbetter of them.\\nBut what if we have it all wrong? What if the rst step to greatness is not to\\nstrategize? What if true greatness begins with a pause\\u2014w ith forming a deep\\nrelationship with our own inner truth?\\nMany of us are disconnected from what goes on within. There is a\\nfundamental error we make in recognizing the truth of our inner states. Often\\nwe mistake inner stress for passion, worry for love, anger for inspiration, and fear\\nfor intelligence.\\nI have seen many people experience quite a shock once they learn to clearly\\nidentify their inner states. They are surprised to discover they\\u2019ve been clinging to\\nstressful emotions despite logically knowing that it is unwise. They\\u2019ve become\\naccustomed to disturbed states because they either didn\\u2019t know how to climb\\nout of them or they simply couldn\\u2019t imagine another way of being.\\nWhen we are far removed from what we feel, we can easily mistake suering\\nstates for motivating or intelligent states. For instance, some of us use anger or\\nstress as a way to drive ourselves. We see our rage or our anxiety as a tool. When\\nwe look back upon some of the gains while in this state, it\\u2019s easy to become\\naddicted to our anger and dependent upon our frustration. We do not believe\\nwe can create or achieve success without it.\\nThen there are those of us who are addicted to worrying. The only way we\\nknow how to show love is by obsessing about our loved ones\\u2019 health, future, or\\nachievements. In many families, this is how parents express their love for their\\nchildren. And it\\u2019s the way many of us learn to show love to our own partners,\\nfriends, and children.\\nBut it is a suering state of consciousness all the same.\\nStill others have been taught to focus more on other people\\u2019s inner states\\nthan their own. Even if they do not outwardly blame others for their discomfort,\\ntheir rst instinct is to try to understand others instead of themselves. As caring\\nas this approach may seem, if you are unable to connect with yourself, you will\\nnever connect with another.\\nWhat happens when we are driven by such states of consciousness? We might\\nmanage to be successful, but the journey will be so fraught that it will come at a\\ngreat cost. We make enemies on our rise, damage our health, or are simply\\nunable to enjoy what we created. Nor will we allow others around us to enjoy\\nthemselves. If we believe stress and anxiety are motivating, we will keep our\\nteams and families stressed right along with us.\\nSociety certainly perpetuates ideas such as suering is a blessing and struggle\\nis the key to success. Think of how much we revere the idea of the \\u201ctortured\\nartist\\u201d or the leaders who \\u201csuered their way to the top.\\u201d But what if suering\\nhad nothing to do with the success of these individuals? What if, in fact, it\\nprevented so many of our heroes and geniuses from reaping the benets of their\\ngreatest achievements? What if it is \\u201cfreedom from suering\\u201d that actually led\\nthese people to greatness, and nobody realized this?\\nSo what is inner truth?\\nInner Truth: A Light in the Dark\\nInner truth is not a confession you make to another. It is not the policy of\\nhonest speech. It is much deeper and more powerful.\\nInner truth is an awareness and more than an awareness. It is a\\nnonjudgmental observation of what is happening within you while you ponder\\ntwo deep insights about suering\\u2014insights that you may remember from\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

["langchain", "schema", "messages", "HumanMessage"], "kwargs": {"content": "---Task---\\nExtract entities and relationships from the input text in Data to be Processed below.\\n\\n---Instructions---\\n1.  **Strict Adherence to Format:** Strictly adhere to all format requirements for entity and relationship lists, including output order, field delimiters, and proper noun handling, as specified in the system prompt.\\n2.  **Output Content Only:** Output *only* the extracted list of entities and relationships. Do not include any introductory or concluding remarks, explanations, or additional text before or after the list.\\n3.  **Completion Signal:** Output `<|COMPLETE|>` as the final line after all relevant entities and relationships have been extracted and presented.\\n4.  **Output Language:** Ensure the output language is English. Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) must be kept in their original language and not translated.\\n\\n---Data to be Processed---\\n<Entity_types>\\n[Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject]\\n\\n<Input Text>\\n```\\nThe Second Life Journey: Dissolve\\nthe Inner Divide\\nBy Preethaji\\nWhen our daughter, Lokaa, was ve years old, her English tutor gave her a poem\\ntitled \\u201cInside-Outside,\\u201d by Abigail Grith, that began:\\nMy inside self and my outside self\\nAre di\\ufb00erent as can be\\nThe poem went on to describe all the ways in which the character\\u2019s physical\\nappearance was at odds with the way she felt about herself.\\nThe tutor then asked Lokaa to write her own version of the sample poem.\\nTwenty-ve minutes later, with Lokaa no closer to writing a poem like the\\nsample, the tutor handed her poem to me and said, \\u201cLokaa\\u2019s poem is not like the\\nsample poem; you will nevertheless like what she has written anyway,\\u201d and\\nwalked away.\\nHere\\u2019s what Lokaa wrote:\\nI am pretty\\nas a buzzing bee\\nI don\\u2019t want to be anyone\\ndi\\ufb00erent than me.\\nI\\u2019m smart and intelligent,\\nSweet and kind,\\nHow should I change\\nwhat is already \\ufb01ne?\\nI want to be a friend\\nto all who are good\\nI am me\\nand you are you!\\nThe model poem portrayed an individual with two inner voices, while Lokaa\\ndid not have that inner divide. When people met Lokaa, they witnessed someone\\nin a beautiful state\\u2014but that experience was not unique to her childhood. She\\ncontinues to have that same inner clarity and non-conicted state of being even\\ntoday.\\nPerhaps many of us were like this as children. We have all hugged a loved one\\nwholly and unselfconsciously. We\\u2019ve delighted in the feel of a dog\\u2019s fur or the\\ntaste of fruit. We\\u2019ve contemplated the rainbows the sun makes in a water\\ndroplet. The simple pleasures of human life were a wonder to our senses. We felt\\njoyful, complete, whole.\\nSomewhere along the line, many of us became divided, conicted individuals\\nand self-obsession set in. In many ways our society accentuates this feeling of\\ndivision. For those of us educated in a system based on test scores and rankings,\\nwe learned to compare, compete, and judge. We were taught to see peers not as\\nfriends but as competitors.\\nThe battle we were ghting was not just against other people. We were also\\ntaught to wage war with ourselves. We began to paint a picture in our minds of\\nthe person we wanted to be and we would become frustrated when we fell short\\nof our expectations. We unconsciously became either pleasers or provers. As\\npleasers, we live in fear of others being displeased with us and therefore make our\\nchoices and actions to appease them. As provers, we remember our past hurts,\\nhold angry conversations in our minds, and make our life choices to prove the\\npeople who hurt us wrong. We become so accustomed to life during wartime\\nthat we forget there is another way to be.\\nBut how is it that we became so disconnected from our beautiful state of\\nwholeness in the rst place? How did we cease to be the beautiful self we truly\\nare? How did we become such self-preoccupied individuals?\\nWhy Am I Unhappy?\\nWho is the rst person you wake up to every morning? Who is the person you\\nspend every moment of your life with, even in your dreams? Who is it you are\\nwith when you are alone or when you keep company?\\nYou.\\nDo you love that you? Are you caring to that you? Or are you critical and\\njudgmental of that you? Are you your own best friend?\\nThink about what happens when you are angry with a friend, a family\\nmember, or a colleague. You try to change them. You give them advice or you tell\\nthem you won\\u2019t stand for their behavior. You may even pray for them to change.\\nIf they refuse, you may distance yourself from them. You make fewer plans with\\nthem, and you are slower to return their phone calls. If things become\\nunbearable, you might even sever the relationship altogether.\\nBut what if the person you are dissatised with is yourself?\\nWhat if the person you do not like is yourself?\\nWhat if the person you absolutely hate is you?\\nPlease pause here. Inhale deeply and slowly exhale a couple of times. Quietly\\nshine the spotlight of attention on your relationship with yourself. Bring your\\nattention to moments when you felt a beautiful state of care and respect for\\nyourself. Breathe deeply and stay in a state of observation of those moments.\\nNow bring your attention to moments when you were in suffering states of\\ndissatisfaction or dislike for yourself. Breathe deeply and observe your state in\\nthose moments.\\nHopefully, you have had some moments when you felt a beautiful\\nconnection to yourself. You simply loved yourself as you are.\\nThere have likely also been times when you felt pain or discomfort within. In\\nsuch moments you may have either sought out external solutions and escapes or\\naccepted the inner war as \\u201cnormal,\\u201d forgetting that you are not this warring self;\\nyou are a beautiful self. You are not a sheep fearing your survival in the jungle;\\nyou are a lion.\\nIf we don\\u2019t have a beautiful relationship with ourselves, everything about us\\n\\u2014th e way we walk, the way we talk, our speech, our way of thinking, and our\\neorts at success\\u2014will be tainted with a nagging sense of self-doubt. What can\\nwe possibly achieve when we are stuck in this suering state? We need to dissolve\\nit.\\nUnable to cope with the ever-widening internal divide, many people\\ndesperately try to chisel and re-chisel their bodies. Millions take to narcotics,\\ndrink alcohol, or even contemplate or commit suicide.\\nAnd yet, for all our grasping at external solutions to changing how we feel\\nabout ourselves, there is no evidence that any human being has ever found\\nhappiness while at war with oneself. After all, if you spend the majority of your\\ntime in this warring state, how much energy will you have left to enjoy your\\nrelationships, wealth, leisure, or success?\\nWhat else happens when we are not at peace with ourselves?\\nDid you ever play tag as a child? The way I played the game was to stand in a\\ncircle and say, \\u201cEeny meeny miney mo, catch a tiger by the toe\\u00a0.\\u00a0.\\u00a0.\\u00a0,\\u201d then\\neliminate one kid after the other until the last one left was the chaser.\\nWhat were we doing? Instead of making a clear choice, we allowed luck to\\ndecide. No one needed to take responsibility for the selection of the chaser.\\nIf we are not harmonious within ourselves, quite often that is how we\\napproach the most important decisions of our lives, even as adults. We are\\nindecisive because our suering states take away faith and respect for ourselves\\nand our decisions. We continue to play \\u201ceeny meeny miney mo\\u201d in the process of\\nselecting our jobs, our spouses, or our business partners. We simply cannot\\ncondently decide or choose.\\nEven if\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

Instead of making a clear choice, we allowed luck to\\ndecide. We are\\nindecisive because our suering states take away faith and respect for ourselves\\nand our decisions. We continue to play \\u201ceeny meeny miney mo\\u201d in the process of\\nselecting our jobs, our spouses, or our business partners. We simply cannot\\ncondently decide or choose.\\nEven if we do choose, we continue to doubt. We doubt that we are in love\\nwith the right person, sometimes even after three years of being in a relationship.\\nWe doubt our choice of career a decade into the job. We doubt our course of\\nstudy in college long after graduation. When we are lost in the clash of\\nconicting views and opinions, we forget that life can be beautiful indeed.\\nUnable to bear this inner chaos, we run from one quick x to the next,\\nlooking for anything to silence the conicting and nagging voices that create this\\ninner claustrophobia. And when nothing leads to any lasting change, it can seem\\nas if the world has given us a raw deal.\\n\\u201cBut I\\u2019m a good person!\\u201d we cry. \\u201cI have never harmed anyone in my life. So\\nwhy am I so unhappy?\\u201d\\nThree Expressions of the Warring Self\\nIn the famous epic Indian poem Ramayana, the villain, King Ravana, has a\\nunique dilemma. He was not a foolish or evil king, like so many of the villains\\nwe\\u2019ve come to know. Ravana was a great scholar. He was well versed in the\\nscriptures, and he created great prosperity for his kingdom.\\nSo how did the actions of this man, considered virtuous in every way, lead to\\nthe death of his brother, his son, and his entire clan? What led him to kidnap the\\nwife of the hero, Rama\\u2014an action that led to Ravana\\u2019s entire kingdom going\\ndown in ames? How did such an erudite man become so destructive?\\nThe story describes Ravana as a man with ten heads. His many heads were\\nsymbolic of his dissenting values and obsessive desires that kept him trapped in\\nhis own head. None of his knowledge was any help when it came to silencing the\\ntorment of his conicting desires and values. This was a man at war with\\nhimself, and it did not take long for that inner war to infect everyone around\\nhim.\\nWhen you read the story of Ravana, you might nd yourself asking a\\nquestion that is as relevant today as it was in ancient times:\\nWhy do good people become bad?\\nHave we not all asked this question at some point in our lives? We look at a\\nsister, son, or friend who has lost their way and we wonder: What on earth went\\nwrong? We look at the leader or the artist we once believed in and ask ourselves:\\nHow did they go so far o\\ufb00 track?\\nWhen a person is lost in a state of inner war, they become like Ravana. Not\\nonly are they self-destructive, but they have the power to destroy others. A\\nperson could be the most well-intentioned in a crowd of hundreds, but if their\\ninner self is a battleground of conicting values, they will bring chaos to their\\nworld.\\nThe re of our inner war can be sparked by conicting desires, such as:\\nI want to be a sel\\ufb02ess mother, but then I\\u2019ll have to give up my career.\\u00a0.\\u00a0.\\u00a0. I can\\u2019t\\nhave it all.\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')

Your intuition is awake.\\nThe insight at the heart of this book is simple enough: there are only two\\nstates of being, a suering state and a beautiful state. One state creates an energy\\neld of chaos around you. The other welcomes harmonious happening into\\nyour life. Therefore, the most important choice any of us can ever make is \\u201cIn\\nwhich state do I want to live?\\u201d\\nBut this idea also presents a question: Once we make the choice, can we walk\\naround in a beautiful state all the time?\\nNo, we cannot. A choice alone is not enough. We must rst understand that\\nsuering states are often unconscious and deeply ingrained. They took root in us\\neither epigenetically, prenatally, in our early childhoods, or even in our later\\nyears.\\nOur suering states prevent us from feeling this sense of wholeness, calm, joy,\\nand\\n```\\n\\n<Output>", "type": "human"}}]')
